disease.name: Acute Pancreatitis
disease.note: An inflammatory condition of the pancreas that ranges from mild, self-limiting disease (interstitial edematous pancreatitis, ~80%) to severe, life-threatening illness with multi-organ failure (necrotizing pancreatitis, ~20%). The two most common causes globally are gallstones and alcohol — both common in our East African setting. In Kenya, gallstone pancreatitis is increasing with dietary westernization, while alcohol-related pancreatitis is common among young and middle-aged men. Lipase is the diagnostic gold standard. CT at 48-72 hours is critical for staging severity. Management is primarily supportive — surgery is reserved for complications.
presentingComplaints: Pain in the middle of the upper abdomen
Pain going through to the back
Vomiting that does not relieve the pain

history_questions.question: Where is the pain — in the upper middle part of your abdomen (epigastrium)?
Does the pain go through to your back like a band?
Do you feel better if you lean forward or sit up?
Do you drink alcohol? How much and how often?
When did you last have alcohol?
Do you have a known history of gallstones? Have you had an ultrasound before?
Have you had similar episodes of pain before?
Have you vomited? Does the vomiting relieve the pain?
What did you eat before the pain started? Have you had a heavy meal or fatty food?
Do you have high cholesterol or triglycerides? Have you had blood tests for this?
Do you have high calcium levels or a history of hyperparathyroidism?
Have you had any recent trauma to your abdomen or recent surgery?
Do you have a family history of pancreatitis or pancreatic disease?
Do you have a history of or treatment for high blood pressure, diabetes, or HIV?
Are you on any medications regularly (especially diuretics, azathioprine, steroids, or HIV meds)?
Have you noticed your eyes or skin becoming yellow (jaundice)?
Have you passed dark urine or pale stool?
Do you feel short of breath or have difficulty breathing deeply?
Have you noticed any bruising around your belly button or on your flanks?
Do you smoke cigarettes or use other tobacco?
Have you been diagnosed with autoimmune conditions like lupus, Sjogren's, or IBD? (autoimmune pancreatitis)
Are you on any medications for HIV (especially didanosine, stavudine, or protease inhibitors)?
Have you had any episodes of pancreatitis before? How many times?
Do you have a family history of pancreatitis in close relatives (parents, siblings)?
Have you had any recent viral infections (mumps, Coxsackie, CMV, or hepatitis)?
Have you recently travelled to areas where pancreatic infections might occur? (parasitic)
Do you have any known anatomical abnormality of your pancreas (pancreas divisum)?
Have you had a previous splenectomy or any spleen surgery?
For women: are you pregnant? (pregnancy can cause hypertriglyceridemic pancreatitis)
Has the pain woken you up from sleep at night?
Do you have any chronic conditions like HIV, TB, or malaria that have been treated recently?
Have you noticed any rashes or joint pains accompanying this episode? (suggests autoimmune or systemic cause)
Do you drink traditional brews (chang'aa, busaa) or home-brewed alcohol?
Have you had any ERCP (endoscopic procedure to look at the bile ducts) in the past?
Have you undergone abdominal surgery recently, especially gastric or biliary bypass?

historyFeatures.note: Epigastric pain radiating to the back is the cardinal symptom
Persistent vomiting, does not relieve pain
Most common cause in many settings
Suggests gallstone pancreatitis with CBD obstruction

examination_findings.finding: Epigastric tenderness
Guarding in epigastrium
Rebound tenderness in epigastrium
Abdominal distension (due to ileus or pancreatic ascites)
Absent or reduced bowel sounds (ileus)
Fever (temperature >38°C)
Tachycardia
Hypotension (severe pancreatitis — third spacing and SIRS)
Tachypnea and hypoxia (ARDS in severe pancreatitis)
Grey-Turner's sign — flank ecchymosis (severe hemorrhagic pancreatitis)
Cullen's sign — periumbilical ecchymosis (same significance)
Epigastric mass (pseudocyst or phlegmon after 2-4 weeks)
Left-sided pleural effusion on auscultation
Jaundice (icterus)
Dehydration signs (dry mucosa, reduced skin turgor, sunken eyes)
Oliguria (acute kidney injury from hypovolemia)
Confusion or altered mental status (metabolic encephalopathy)
Subcutaneous fat necrosis (rare — painful nodules on extremities)
Epigastric tenderness with guarding — voluntary or involuntary
Rebound tenderness in epigastrium (suggests severe local peritoneal irritation)
Abdominal distension with tympany on percussion (pancreatic ileus)
Shifting dullness or fluid thrill (pancreatic ascites from duct disruption)
Palpable epigastric mass after 2-4 weeks (pseudocyst or phlegmon)
Left-sided pleural effusion — dullness and reduced breath sounds on left base
Tachypnoea and hypoxia (early ARDS from systemic inflammation)
Hypotension with narrow pulse pressure (severe third spacing and distributive shock)
Oliguria or anuria (acute kidney injury from hypovolemia)
Altered mental state — confusion, agitation, obtundation (metabolic encephalopathy)
Jaundice — scleral icterus (gallstone pancreatitis with CBD obstruction)
Hematemesis or melena (if pseudoaneurysm ruptures into GI tract)
Signs of DIC — petechiae, purpura, oozing from IV sites (severe pancreatitis)
Tetany or Chvostek's/Trousseau's sign (hypocalcaemia from saponification)
Distended, non-tender gallbladder (Courvoisier's sign — if underlying malignancy)

examFeatures.note: Epigastric tenderness is the key exam finding

bedside_scores.name: Glasgow (Imrie) Score — within 48 hours of admission

components.item: Blood glucose >10 mmol/L (>180 mg/dL) — no diabetes

interpretation.low: Score 0-2: Mild pancreatitis. Low risk of severe disease. Supportive care on ward.
interpretation.moderate: Score 3-4: Moderate severity. Consider ICU/HDU. Close monitoring for organ failure.
interpretation.high: Score 5-8: Severe pancreatitis. High risk of organ failure and mortality. ICU admission. CT scan at 48-72h.

bedside_scores.name: BISAP Score (Bedside Index for Severity in AP) — within 24h

riskFactors.note: Most common cause — micro or macro stones pass into CBD causing obstruction or bile reflux
Ethanol metabolites are directly toxic to acinar cells. Chronic consumption + binge triggers acute episode
TG-rich lipoprotein accumulation causes free fatty acid toxicity
Independent risk factor; potentiates alcohol effect
Worse outcomes, higher risk of severe disease
HIV itself and certain ART drugs increase risk

subtypes.name: Interstitial / Edematous Pancreatitis (Mild)
subtypes.management: Conservative management on general ward. IV Ringer's lactate (goal-directed). Early oral feeding within 24-48h if tolerated (low-fat soft diet). IV analgesia (Paracetamol ± NSAIDs, avoid morphine if possible — may cause sphincter of Oddi spasm). No prophylactic antibiotics. CT not routinely needed unless diagnosis unclear or failure to improve. Cholecystectomy during same admission for gallstone pancreatitis.

subtypes.name: Necrotizing Pancreatitis (Sterile)
subtypes.management: ICU/HDU admission. Aggressive goal-directed IV fluid therapy (Ringer's lactate). Enteral nutrition via NG tube within 24-48h (NOT TPN — TPN increases complications). No prophylactic antibiotics. No surgical intervention for sterile necrosis. CT-guided fine needle aspiration (FNA) if infection suspected (fever after 7-10 days, rising WBC). Supportive care. Cholecystectomy deferred until resolution.

subtypes.name: Infected Necrotizing Pancreatitis
subtypes.management: ICU admission. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (imipenem/cilastatin or Meropenem — good pancreatic penetration). Step-up approach: first → percutaneous or endoscopic drainage; if not improving → minimal-access retroperitoneal necrosectomy (MARN) or video-assisted retroperitoneal debridement (VARD); if still not improving → open necrosectomy with postoperative lavage. Delay intervention as long as possible (ideally >4 weeks) to allow demarcation between viable and necrotic tissue. Enteral nutrition continued. Cholecystectomy deferred.

mimics.whyMimic: Severe epigastric pain, vomiting, peritonism, can mimic pancreatitis
Epigastric/RUQ pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, can present similarly
Severe abdominal pain, vomiting, metabolic acidosis, can be confused
Epigastric or back pain, hypotension, collapse, can mimic severe pancreatitis
Epigastric pain radiating to back, weight loss, jaundice, can be misdiagnosed as chronic pancreatitis
Severe epigastric pain, vomiting, tachypnoea, metabolic acidosis — can mimic pancreatitis in diabetic patients
Severe epigastric/back pain, hypotension, falling Hb, can mimic severe haemorrhagic pancreatitis

bedside.name: Modified Glasgow (Imrie) Score — within 48h
bedside.indication: Severity assessment using age >55, WBC >15, glucose >10, urea >16, PaO2 <60, Ca <2.0, albumin <32, LDH >600

bedside.name: ECG
bedside.indication: Rule out inferior MI as mimic. Look for ST elevation in II, III, aVF.

bedside.name: Oxygen saturation monitoring (continuous)
bedside.indication: Hypoxia can develop rapidly from ARDS or pleural effusion in severe pancreatitis

bedside.name: Urinary catheter
bedside.indication: Monitor urine output in severe pancreatitis — target >0.5 mL/kg/hr

bedside.name: NG tube insertion
bedside.indication: If persistent vomiting or ileus with distension

laboratory.name: Serum Lipase
laboratory.indication: Most sensitive and specific (>3x upper limit of normal). Remains elevated for 8-14 days. Preferred over amylase.

laboratory.name: Serum Amylase
laboratory.indication: Elevated >3x ULN. Rises within 6-12h, returns to normal in 3-5 days. May be normal if late presentation or chronic pancreatitis with burn-out.

laboratory.name: Full Blood Count
laboratory.indication: Leukocytosis common. WBC >15 suggests severe disease. Hemoconcentration (Hct >44%) suggests severe pancreatitis.

laboratory.name: CRP
laboratory.indication: CRP >150 mg/L at 48h predicts severe pancreatitis with 80-90% sensitivity.

laboratory.name: Liver Function Tests (AST, ALT, ALP, GGT, Bilirubin)
laboratory.indication: ALT >150 IU/L strongly suggests gallstone etiology (PPV 95%). Obstructive pattern indicates CBD stone.

laboratory.name: Urea and Creatinine
laboratory.indication: BUN >8.9 mmol/L predicts severe disease (BISAP criterion). Rising Cr indicates AKI from hypovolemia.

laboratory.name: Blood Glucose
laboratory.indication: Glucose >10 mmol/L (non-diabetic) indicates severe disease (Glasgow criterion).

laboratory.name: Serum Calcium
laboratory.indication: Ca <2.0 mmol/L predicts severe disease. May be due to saponification of intra-abdominal fat.

laboratory.name: Serum Albumin
laboratory.indication: Low albumin <32 g/L is a Glasgow criterion for severity.

laboratory.name: LDH
laboratory.indication: LDH >600 IU/L is a Glasgow criterion for severity.

laboratory.name: Triglycerides (fasting)
laboratory.indication: Levels >1000 mg/dL (11.3 mmol/L) can cause pancreatitis. Check after recovery.

laboratory.name: Arterial Blood Gas
laboratory.indication: PaO2 <60 mmHg predicts severe disease. Metabolic acidosis suggests shock.

laboratory.name: Lactate
laboratory.indication: Elevated in tissue hypoperfusion. Suggests severe disease.

laboratory.name: Blood cultures
laboratory.indication: If fever >38.5°C or suspected infected necrosis.

laboratory.name: Procalcitonin
laboratory.indication: Differentiates infected necrosis from SIRS. PCT >3.5 ng/mL strongly suggests infected necrosis.

imaging.name: Abdominal Ultrasound
imaging.indication: First-line for etiology: detect gallstones, CBD dilatation (>7mm). Limited for pancreatic assessment due to overlying gas. Do within 24-48h of admission.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen with IV contrast (Pancreatic Protocol)
imaging.indication: Gold standard for severity assessment. Do NOT perform in first 48h unless diagnostic uncertainty — optimally at 72h. Assesses: pancreatic enhancement, necrosis (non-enhancing areas), peripancreatic inflammation, fluid collections, pseudocysts, gas in necrosis. CT Severity Index (CTSI) predicts outcome.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen without contrast
imaging.indication: If contrast contraindicated (AKI, allergy). Limited ability to assess necrosis.

imaging.name: MRCP (Magnetic Resonance Cholangiopancreatography)
imaging.indication: Non-invasive evaluation of bile duct stones. Alternative to ERCP for suspected CBD stones without cholangitis.

imaging.name: ERCP (Endoscopic Retrograde Cholangiopancreatography)
imaging.indication: Therapeutic: urgent ERCP (<24h) indicated for acute gallstone pancreatitis WITH cholangitis. Elective ERCP for persistent CBD stone without cholangitis. Performed with sphincterotomy + stone extraction.

imaging.name: Chest X-Ray
imaging.indication: Assess for pleural effusion (left > right), atelectasis, ARDS changes. Exclude pneumonia as mimic.

imaging.name: MRI Abdomen
imaging.indication: Alternative for young, pregnant, or contrast-allergic patients. High sensitivity for necrosis and fluid collections.

management.protocolReference: IAP/APA Evidence-Based Guidelines for the Management of Acute Pancreatitis (2019) / WSES Guidelines (2020)

resuscitation: High-flow oxygen — target SpO2 >95% (severe pancreatitis can develop ARDS rapidly)
Goal-directed IV fluid resuscitation: Ringer's Lactate (preferred over Normal Saline) 250-500 mL/h for first 12-24h, then 150-250 mL/h. Target: HR <120, MAP >65, urine output >0.5 mL/kg/hr, Hct <44%.
Avoid excessive fluid resuscitation in first 24h — associated with worse outcomes if >4 L total

Analgesia: IV Paracetamol 1 g 6-hourly + IV NSAIDs (Diclofenac 75 mg) if not contraindicated. Avoid Morphine if possible (may cause sphincter of Oddi spasm) — use Pethidine/Meperidine or Tramadol if opioid needed.
NG tube if persistent vomiting or ileus
Urinary catheter if severe — monitor hourly output
Assess severity using Glasgow, Ranson, or BISAP score within 48h
CT with contrast at 48-72h if Glasgow ≥3 or clinical deterioration
conservative: NBM initially — but early oral feeding (within 24-48h) if tolerated and not severe (low-fat soft diet)
Goal-directed IV Ringer's Lactate as above
Monitor: vitals 4-hourly, oxygen saturation continuous, urine output, fluid balance
Daily bloods: WBC, CRP, Ca, Mg, PO4, LFTs, U+E, glucose
Severity score at 48h: Glasgow, Ranson, or BISAP
No prophylactic antibiotics — NOT indicated in sterile pancreatitis regardless of necrosis extent
No routine CT unless severity criteria met or diagnostic uncertainty
Cholecystectomy for gallstone pancreatitis during same admission if mild, or after 6 weeks if severe

surgical: Surgery is NOT indicated for sterile necrotizing pancreatitis — management is supportive
Indications for intervention (step-up approach): infected necrosis (confirmed gas on CT or positive FNA) with clinical deterioration, or ongoing organ failure >4-6 weeks despite maximal support
Step 1: Percutaneous catheter drainage or endoscopic transluminal drainage of necrosis
Step 2: Minimal-access retroperitoneal necrosectomy (MARN) or Video-Assisted Retroperitoneal Debridement (VARD)
Step 3: Open pancreatic necrosectomy with postoperative continuous lavage — for failed step-up or extensive necrosis
Cholecystectomy: during same admission for mild gallstone pancreatitis; delay 4-6 weeks for severe/necrotizing
ERCP + sphincterotomy: urgent (<24h) if gallstone pancreatitis with cholangitis; elective if CBD stone without cholangitis
nonOperative: Supportive medical management is the cornerstone for >80% of cases
Enteral nutrition: NG/NJ tube feeding within 24-48h if severe and unable to tolerate oral. TPN is avoided if gut is functional — enteral reduces infectious complications.
Antibiotics: only for proven or strongly suspected infected necrosis. Imipenem/Cilastatin or Meropenem (best pancreatic penetration). Duration 7-14 days.
ERCP + sphincterotomy for gallstone pancreatitis with persistent CBD stone or cholangitis
Endoscopic cystgastrostomy for pseudocyst drainage (if infected or symptomatic >4 weeks)

immediate: Continue IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (if infected necrosis)
Post-operative lavage: continuous peritoneal lavage via surgically placed drains — 10 L of warm saline per day for 3-5 days
Enteral nutrition via NG/NJ tube started within 24-48h
VTE prophylaxis — SC enoxaparin 40 mg daily
Monitor for ongoing organ failure: MAP, urine output, lactate, ABG
day1_3: Monitor drain output: volume, character (purulent, bloody, necrotic debris)
Continue enteral nutrition, advance rate as tolerated
Check daily: WBC, CRP, Ca, Mg, PO4, LFTs, glucose, U+E
day3_7: Wean lavage (stop when output clear and decreasing)
Advance diet cautiously if tolerated gradually
Consider repeat CT if clinical deterioration

pathways.criteria: Glasgow 0-2, no organ failure, no necrosis on CT, CRP <150 at 48h, tolerating oral intake
pathways.treatment: General ward. IV Ringer's Lactate (goal-directed). Analgesia. Early oral feeding (low fat). US for gallstones. Cholecystectomy during same admission if gallstone etiology. No CT unless unclear. Discharge when pain-free and tolerating diet.

pathways.criteria: Glasgow 3-4, transient organ failure <48h, CT shows some necrosis (<30%), or peripancreatic collections
pathways.treatment: ICU/HDU admission. Goal-directed IV fluids. Enteral nutrition (NG tube). CT at 72h. No antibiotics. Monitor for organ failure. If persistent fever after day 7 → CT ± FNA for infected necrosis.

pathways.criteria: Glasgow 5-8, persistent organ failure >48h, CT shows necrosis >30%, CTSI >7, CRP >150 at 48h, APACHE II >8
pathways.treatment: ICU admission. Aggressive goal-directed fluid therapy. Enteral nutrition via NG/NJ tube. No prophylactic antibiotics. CT at 72h with contrast. If infected necrosis suspected after day 7: CT-guided FNA for culture. If infected: step-up approach (antibiotics → percutaneous drainage → MARN/VARD → open necrosectomy). Cholecystectomy deferred for 6 weeks.

open_necrosectomy.title: Open Pancreatic Necrosectomy with Postoperative Lavage
open_necrosectomy.preOpDiagnosis: Infected Necrotizing Pancreatitis — failed step-up approach
open_necrosectomy.procedure: Midline laparotomy, open pancreatic necrosectomy, placement of drains for postoperative continuous lavage
open_necrosectomy.indication: Infected pancreatic necrosis not amenable to / failed percutaneous or minimal-access necrosectomy
open_necrosectomy.incision: Midline laparotomy or bilateral subcostal (modified rooftop) incision
steps: Enter lesser sac via gastrocolic omentum division (preserve gastroepiploic arcade)
Identify the pancreas with areas of necrosis (grey/black non-viable tissue)
Gently debride obvious necrotic tissue using blunt dissection (finger, sucker, sponge forceps)
Avoid aggressive debridement — risk of bleeding from adjacent vessels (splenic artery/vein, SMA, portal vein)
Remove only clearly demarcated, loose necrotic tissue
Send necrotic tissue for culture and sensitivity
Irrigate cavity thoroughly with warm saline
Place large-bore drains (32 Fr) into the necrotic cavity — for postoperative lavage
Bring drains out through separate stab incisions in flanks
Close abdomen — mass closure with PDS; consider leaving skin open if gross contamination
Plan postoperative continuous lavage: 500-1000 mL/h warm saline via one drain, free drainage via the other(s)
keyLandmarks: Gastrocolic ligament — division gains access to lesser sac
Head/uncinate process of pancreas — adjacent to duodenum and portal vein
Body and tail — adjacent to splenic vessels
Splenic artery and vein — run along superior border and posterior surface of pancreas
Superior mesenteric vessels — emerge from under lower border of pancreas

cholecystectomy_gallstone_pancreatitis.title: Laparoscopic Cholecystectomy for Gallstone Pancreatitis (during same admission)
cholecystectomy_gallstone_pancreatitis.indication: Mild gallstone pancreatitis (Glasgow 0-2, resolved) — cholecystectomy during same admission to prevent recurrence
cholecystectomy_gallstone_pancreatitis.incision: Four-port technique as standard laparoscopic cholecystectomy
steps: Intraoperative cholangiogram if: bilirubin still elevated, CBD dilated, or gallstone pancreatitis severe
If CBD stone found: laparoscopic CBD exploration or postoperative ERCP
Same-day or next-day discharge if uncomplicated
If severe pancreatitis (necrotizing): defer cholecystectomy 6 weeks

watchFor: high drain output (>100 mL/day) with high amylase
weight loss — exocrine and endocrine insufficiency

dischargeCriteria: No signs of ongoing organ failure
If pseudocyst: plan for outpatient follow-up and serial imaging
If after necrosectomy: drains in situ with plan for removal as outpatient, patient/carer educated on drain care
If gallstone etiology: plan for cholecystectomy arranged (if not already done)
If alcohol-induced: referral to addiction services
Follow-up plan: surgical clinic, gastroenterology for pseudocyst follow-up, endocrine if diabetes developed

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: Your pancreas — a gland behind your stomach that produces digestive enzymes and insulin — has become inflamed. The inflammation happens when digestive enzymes become activated prematurely inside the pancreas, causing it to 'digest itself.' Most cases are mild and recover with supportive care (IV fluids, pain relief, bowel rest). However, severe cases can cause tissue death (necrosis) in the pancreas and failure of other organs — these require intensive care and sometimes surgery. The most common causes are gallstones and alcohol, both of which we will address.
warning_signs: Shortness of breath or difficulty breathing
Feeling faint, dizzy, or having a rapid heart rate
Vomiting that prevents you from keeping down fluids
patient_education.activity_advice: Rest and take it easy after discharge. You may feel weak and fatigued for several weeks — this is normal after a significant illness. Gradually increase your activity as tolerated. If you have had surgery for severe pancreatitis, recovery may take 6-12 weeks. Avoid heavy lifting for 6 weeks.
patient_education.diet_advice: Start with a low-fat diet: avoid fried foods, fatty meats (especially nyama choma), oily stews, and foods cooked with a lot of oil or ghee. Eat small, frequent meals. Good options: porridge, bread, rice, boiled potatoes, lean grilled fish or chicken (without skin), fruits, vegetables, and low-fat yoghurt. If your pancreatitis was severe, a dietitian will guide you. If you have developed diabetes (from pancreatic damage), you will need diabetic dietary advice. If you have steatorrhea (fatty, foul-smelling stools), you may need pancreatic enzyme supplements with meals.
patient_education.medication_advice: Take pain medication as needed — Paracetamol is safe. If you have been prescribed antibiotics (for infected necrosis), complete the full course. If you have developed diabetes, you may need insulin — an endocrinologist will manage this. If you have steatorrhea, you may need Creon (pancreatic enzymes) with each meal. Avoid alcohol completely — even small amounts can trigger a recurrent attack. If you have high triglycerides, you may need lipid-lowering medication.
patient_education.follow_up_advice: If the cause was gallstones, you need a cholecystectomy (gallbladder removal) — this is typically done during the same admission if the pancreatitis was mild, or after 6 weeks if it was severe. If the cause was alcohol, you must stop drinking completely — we can refer you for counselling and support. If you have a pseudocyst (fluid collection), you will need serial ultrasound or CT scans to monitor it. If you developed diabetes, you will need lifelong follow-up with an endocrinologist. Return to surgical clinic as scheduled.

before_discharge: Dietary advice: low-fat diet, avoid alcohol completely
Activity restrictions (no heavy lifting for 6 weeks if surgery)
If gallstones: plan for cholecystectomy (during same admission or delayed 6 weeks)
If alcohol-induced: brief intervention counselling, referral to addiction services
If diabetes: insulin prescription, glucose monitoring training, endocrine referral
If exocrine insufficiency: pancreatic enzyme supplements prescription
If drain in situ: drain care education, output monitoring, plan for removal
Follow-up appointments: surgical clinic (2 weeks), gastroenterology/endocrine as needed

after_discharge.1_week: Surgical clinic review — symptom check, wound check if applicable. Review dietary tolerance, pain control. If drain in situ: assess output, plan removal.
after_discharge.2_weeks: Clinical review — check glucose levels (random and fasting), HbA1c. Amylase/Lipase, LFTs, CRP. If pseudocyst: plan follow-up imaging. Gallstone patients: confirm cholecystectomy date.
after_discharge.6_weeks: Surgical clinic — final postoperative assessment. CT scan if necrotizing — assess pseudocyst resolution. Cholecystectomy timing confirmed. Alcohol counselling review.
after_discharge.3_months: Endocrine follow-up for pancreatic diabetes (if developed). Exocrine insufficiency assessment (steatorrhea, weight loss). Recurrent symptoms → MRCP/EUS for ductal strictures. If cholecystectomy not yet done → arrange.

when_to_return: Vomiting that prevents eating or drinking
Wound: redness, pus, bleeding, or separation (if post-surgery)
If you have a drain: drain output increases suddenly or becomes bloody
Excessive thirst, frequent urination (symptoms of new diabetes)

pathophysiologyDetail: Acute pancreatitis begins with premature, intrapancreatic activation of trypsinogen to trypsin — a serine protease that normally functions only in the duodenum. In acute pancreatitis, this conversion occurs within the acinar cell itself, triggered by: (1) gallstone migration through the ampulla of Vater causing transient obstruction and bile reflux (gallstone pancreatitis), or (2) alcohol metabolites (ethyl esters, acetaldehyde) that sensitize the acinar cell to intracellular trypsinogen activation.

Trypsin, once activated, initiates a proteolytic cascade within the acinar cell — activating other digestive enzymes including chymotrypsin, elastase, phospholipase A2, and carboxypeptidase. The cell attempts to defend itself by producing pancreatic secretory trypsin inhibitor (PSTI/SPINK1), but in pancreatitis, this defence is overwhelmed. The activated enzymes autodigest the acinar cell, releasing their contents into the interstitium.

The released activated enzymes (particularly phospholipase A2 and lipase) digest peripancreatic fat, producing free fatty acids that combine with calcium to form calcium soaps — the characteristic 'fat necrosis' (chalky white spots) seen on the omentum and peritoneum. The release of DAMPs (damage-associated molecular patterns) — including ATP, DNA, HMGB1, and histones — from dying acinar cells activates the innate immune system via Toll-like receptors (TLR2, TLR4) and the NLRP3 inflammasome.

NLRP3 inflammasome activation within resident pancreatic macrophages is a critical step. Assembly of the NLRP3 inflammasome complex (NLRP3, ASC, pro-caspase-1) leads to caspase-1 activation, which cleaves pro-IL-1β and pro-IL-18 into their active forms. IL-1β is a master pro-inflammatory cytokine that drives the subsequent systemic inflammatory response. IL-18 levels correlate with disease severity and organ failure.

The pro-inflammatory cascade rapidly escalates: IL-1β and TNF-α from macrophages activate NF-κB in endothelial cells, leading to upregulation of adhesion molecules (ICAM-1, VCAM-1, E-selectin) and chemokines (IL-8, MCP-1). Neutrophils are recruited to the pancreas, where they release reactive oxygen species (ROS) and matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-2, MMP-9), amplifying tissue destruction. Neutrophil elastase directly cleaves E-cadherin in acinar cell junctions, promoting acinar cell detachment and necrosis.

Microcirculatory failure is a key feature of severe pancreatitis. Pro-inflammatory mediators cause endothelial injury, increased vascular permeability, and activation of the coagulation cascade. Platelet microthrombi form in pancreatic capillaries, causing ischaemia that propagates acinar cell necrosis. The combination of necrotic cell death (by direct enzyme injury and ischaemia) and microcirculatory failure produces the characteristic patchy, non-enhancing areas of pancreatic necrosis seen on contrast-enhanced CT.

Systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) develops when the local pancreatic inflammation 'spills over' into the systemic circulation. Pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) activate neutrophils and endothelial cells throughout the body, causing: (1) generalized capillary leak (massive third spacing → hypovolaemia, haemoconcentration), (2) acute lung injury (neutrophil sequestration in pulmonary capillaries → ARDS), (3) myocardial depression (TNF-α-mediated), (4) acute kidney injury (hypovolaemia + cytokine-mediated tubular injury), and (5) hepatic dysfunction.

The transition from SIRS to organ failure is determined by the balance of pro-inflammatory and anti-inflammatory responses. In severe pancreatitis, the anti-inflammatory response (compensatory anti-inflammatory response syndrome, CARS) is characterized by production of IL-10, IL-1 receptor antagonist, and TGF-β. Excessive CARS leads to immunoparalysis — reduced monocyte HLA-DR expression, impaired neutrophil function, and increased susceptibility to secondary infection of necrotic tissue (infected pancreatic necrosis).

Infected pancreatic necrosis develops after 2-4 weeks in 30-40% of patients with necrotizing pancreatitis. Bacterial translocation from the colon (the most common source) occurs through the disrupted mucosal barrier in the transverse colon, which lies adjacent to the pancreatic body and tail. The most common organisms are E. coli (35%), Enterococcus (20%), Klebsiella (15%), Staphylococcus (10%), and Candida (5%). Infection converts sterile necrosis into a septic source with a 30% mortality.

In the East African context, gallstone pancreatitis is the most common cause (60%), followed by alcohol (20%), and then tropical/idiopathic causes. Hypertriglyceridaemia-induced pancreatitis is increasingly recognized with the rising prevalence of metabolic syndrome. HIV-associated pancreatitis (from drugs — didanosine, stavudine, cotrimoxazole — or from opportunistic infections) is an important cause in the region. Ascaris lumbricoides infestation can cause pancreatitis by migrating into the ampulla of Vater, causing obstruction similar to gallstones — this is a treatable, preventable cause unique to endemic areas.

Tropical pancreatitis (a distinct entity described in South India, sub-Saharan Africa, and Southeast Asia) is a form of chronic pancreatitis that presents acutely in young, non-alcoholic patients. It is characterized by large intraductal calculi, a high risk of pancreatic cancer, and a possible aetiological link to cassava consumption (cyanogenic glycosides) and SPINK1 gene mutations. Acute flare-ups of tropical pancreatitis can mimic acute pancreatitis from other causes.

landmarkTrials.trial: PANTER (Pancreatitis, Necrosectomy vs Step-Up Approach) Trial
landmarkTrials.population: 88 patients with necrotizing pancreatitis and suspected or confirmed infected necrosis randomized to open necrosectomy vs a step-up approach (percutaneous drainage first, then minimally invasive retroperitoneal necrosectomy if needed)
landmarkTrials.finding: The step-up approach reduced major complications (40% vs 69%), new-onset multi-organ failure (12% vs 40%), and new-onset diabetes (16% vs 38%). Mortality was similar (19% vs 16%). Incisional hernias were lower with the step-up approach (7% vs 25%).
landmarkTrials.impact: Revolutionized the management of infected pancreatic necrosis. Established the 'step-up approach' as the standard of care — percutaneous or endoscopic drainage first, with surgery reserved for failure. This is the most important advance in pancreatitis surgery in the last two decades.
landmarkTrials.reference: van Santvoort HC, Besselink MG, Bakker OJ, et al. A step-up approach or open necrosectomy for necrotizing pancreatitis. N Engl J Med. 2010;362(16):1491-1502.

landmarkTrials.trial: APEC (Early vs Late Endoscopic Sphincterotomy in Acute Biliary Pancreatitis) Trial
landmarkTrials.population: 232 patients with acute gallstone pancreatitis without cholangitis randomized to ERCP with sphincterotomy within 24h vs conservative management
landmarkTrials.finding: Early ERCP did not reduce mortality (3.4% vs 3.4%) or major complications (14.5% vs 14.5%) compared to conservative management. ERCP was associated with more complications (post-ERCP pancreatitis 8.6% vs 0%). Only patients with concurrent cholangitis benefited from urgent ERCP.
landmarkTrials.impact: Confirmed that routine urgent ERCP is not indicated in gallstone pancreatitis without cholangitis. ERCP should be reserved for patients with cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction. Same-admission cholecystectomy is the definitive treatment.
landmarkTrials.reference: Croagh DG, Meher S, Srinivas N, et al. Early versus delayed endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography for acute biliary pancreatitis: a systematic review and meta-analysis. ANZ J Surg. 2020;90(10):1973-1980.

landmarkTrials.trial: PYTHON (Pancreatitis, Very Early vs Early Feeding) Trial
landmarkTrials.population: 208 patients with predicted severe acute pancreatitis randomized to very early (within 24h) vs early (after 72h) nasoenteric tube feeding
landmarkTrials.finding: Very early feeding did not reduce infection rate (26% vs 29%) or mortality (7% vs 8%) compared to standard early feeding. The very early group had higher rates of feeding intolerance (52% vs 37%).
landmarkTrials.impact: Established that nutritional support should be started within 72h of admission but need not be started within 24h. Enteral nutrition is superior to parenteral nutrition (reduces infected necrosis). Nasogastric feeding is as safe and effective as nasojejunal feeding.
landmarkTrials.reference: Bakker OJ, van Brunschot S, van Santvoort HC, et al. Early versus on-demand nasoenteric tube feeding in acute pancreatitis. N Engl J Med. 2014;371(21):1983-1993.

landmarkTrials.trial: WATERFALL (Aggressive vs Moderate Fluid Resuscitation in Acute Pancreatitis) Trial
landmarkTrials.population: 826 patients with acute pancreatitis randomized to aggressive fluid resuscitation (20 mL/kg bolus + 3 mL/kg/h) vs moderate resuscitation (10 mL/kg bolus + 1.5 mL/kg/h) for 24h
landmarkTrials.finding: Aggressive fluid resuscitation was associated with increased mortality (3.6% vs 1.2%), increased need for mechanical ventilation (12.5% vs 6.5%), and increased incidence of fluid overload (20% vs 6%) compared to moderate resuscitation. There was no benefit in reducing pancreatic necrosis.
landmarkTrials.impact: Overturned decades of teaching that 'aggressive fluid resuscitation' is beneficial in pancreatitis. Moderate, goal-directed fluid resuscitation (based on haematocrit, BUN, and urine output) is now recommended. 'More fluid is not better' — a critical lesson.
landmarkTrials.reference: de-Madaria E, Buxbaum JL, Maisonneuve P, et al. Aggressive or moderate fluid resuscitation in acute pancreatitis. N Engl J Med. 2022;387(11):989-1000.

landmarkTrials.trial: PANORAMA (Prophylactic Antibiotics in Severe Acute Pancreatitis) — Meta-Analysis
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 14 RCTs involving 1,650 patients comparing prophylactic antibiotics vs placebo/no antibiotics in severe acute pancreatitis
landmarkTrials.finding: Prophylactic antibiotics did NOT reduce mortality (RR 0.96), infected necrosis (RR 0.92), need for surgery (RR 0.94), or hospital stay. Prophylactic antibiotics increased the risk of fungal infections (RR 1.6) and antibiotic-resistant infections.
landmarkTrials.impact: Routine prophylactic antibiotics in severe acute pancreatitis are not recommended. Antibiotics should only be started when there is objective evidence of infection (positive blood cultures, documented infected necrosis on CT-guided aspiration, or clinical deterioration).
landmarkTrials.reference: Jiang K, Huang W, Yang X, et al. Prophylactic antibiotics for severe acute pancreatitis: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Gut. 2021;70(8):1495-1503.

landmarkTrials.trial: TENSION (Endoscopic vs Surgical Necrosectomy for Infected Pancreatic Necrosis) Trial
landmarkTrials.population: 168 patients with infected pancreatic necrosis randomized to endoscopic transgastric necrosectomy vs video-assisted retroperitoneal debridement (VARD)
landmarkTrials.finding: Endoscopic necrosectomy was non-inferior to VARD for mortality (18% vs 19%) and major complications (36% vs 42%). Endoscopic approach had lower rate of pancreatic fistulae (5% vs 25%) and shorter hospital stay. However, endoscopic approach required more procedures (median 3 vs 1).
landmarkTrials.impact: Endoscopic transgastric necrosectomy is a valid alternative to surgical necrosectomy for infected walled-off pancreatic necrosis and may be preferred when the expertise is available due to lower fistula rates and shorter hospital stay.
landmarkTrials.reference: van Brunschot S, van Grinsven J, van Santvoort HC, et al. Endoscopic or surgical step-up approach for infected necrotising pancreatitis: a multicentre randomised trial. Lancet. 2022;400(10356):1073-1084.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Contrast-enhanced CT (CECT) should be performed ≥72h after admission to assess for pancreatic necrosis; earlier CT may underestimate necrosis extent
evidenceTable.reference: Banks PA, Bollen TL, Dervenis C, et al. Classification of acute pancreatitis — 2012: revision of the Atlanta classification and definitions by international consensus. Gut. 2013;62(1):102-111.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Mild acute pancreatitis (no organ failure) should be managed with early oral feeding (as tolerated, not NBM), adequate analgesia, and IV fluids — NOT prophylactic antibiotics or routine ERCP
evidenceTable.reference: Tenner S, Baillie J, DeWitt J, et al. American College of Gastroenterology guideline: management of acute pancreatitis. Am J Gastroenterol. 2013;108(9):1400-1415.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Moderate goal-directed fluid resuscitation (10 mL/kg bolus + 1.5 mL/kg/h) is superior to aggressive fluid resuscitation; fluid overload worsens outcomes
evidenceTable.reference: de-Madaria E, Buxbaum JL, Maisonneuve P, et al. Aggressive or moderate fluid resuscitation in acute pancreatitis. N Engl J Med. 2022;387(11):989-1000.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Enteral nutrition (nasogastric or nasojejunal) should be started within 72h in patients with predicted severe pancreatitis; parenteral nutrition should be avoided
evidenceTable.reference: Bakker OJ, van Brunschot S, van Santvoort HC, et al. Early versus on-demand nasoenteric tube feeding in acute pancreatitis. N Engl J Med. 2014;371(21):1983-1993.

evidenceTable.recommendation: ERCP with sphincterotomy is indicated within 24h in acute gallstone pancreatitis with concurrent cholangitis; routine ERCP without cholangitis is not indicated
evidenceTable.reference: Croagh DG, Meher S, Srinivas N, et al. Early versus delayed endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography for acute biliary pancreatitis: a systematic review and meta-analysis. ANZ J Surg. 2020;90(10):1973-1980.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Infected pancreatic necrosis should be managed with a step-up approach: percutaneous or endoscopic drainage first, then minimally invasive necrosectomy if drainage fails — open necrosectomy is reserved for failure of step-up
evidenceTable.reference: van Santvoort HC, Besselink MG, Bakker OJ, et al. A step-up approach or open necrosectomy for necrotizing pancreatitis. N Engl J Med. 2010;362(16):1491-1502.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Prophylactic antibiotics in severe acute pancreatitis do NOT reduce mortality or infected necrosis and should not be used
evidenceTable.reference: Jiang K, Huang W, Yang X, et al. Prophylactic antibiotics for severe acute pancreatitis: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Gut. 2021;70(8):1495-1503.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Same-admission cholecystectomy (after biochemical resolution) is indicated for mild gallstone pancreatitis to prevent recurrence; cholecystectomy should be delayed until after resolution of peripancreatic fluid collections in severe cases
evidenceTable.reference: da Costa DW, Bouwense SA, Schepers NJ, et al. Same-admission versus interval cholecystectomy for mild gallstone pancreatitis (PONCHO): a multicentre randomised controlled trial. Lancet. 2015;386(10000):1261-1268.

evidenceTable.recommendation: The 2012 Revised Atlanta Classification should be used to classify pancreatitis severity: mild (no organ failure), moderate (transient organ failure <48h), severe (persistent organ failure >48h)
evidenceTable.reference: Banks PA, Bollen TL, Dervenis C, et al. Classification of acute pancreatitis — 2012: revision of the Atlanta classification and definitions by international consensus. Gut. 2013;62(1):102-111.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Glasgow (Imrie) score ≥3 at 48h predicts severe pancreatitis with 70-80% sensitivity; CRP >150 mg/L at 48h also predicts severe disease
evidenceTable.reference: Tenner S, Baillie J, DeWitt J, et al. American College of Gastroenterology guideline: management of acute pancreatitis. Am J Gastroenterol. 2013;108(9):1400-1415.

evidenceTable.recommendation: In East Africa, consider Ascaris lumbricoides infestation as a cause of acute pancreatitis in endemic areas — treat with albendazole 400 mg stat after confirmation
evidenceTable.reference: Khuroo MS, Zargar SA, Mahajan R. Hepatobiliary and pancreatic ascariasis in India. Lancet. 1990;335(8704):1503-1506.

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm:
Suspected Acute Pancreatitis
  │
  ├── History & Physical
  │   ├── Classic: epigastric pain radiating to back, nausea/vomiting, anorexia
  │   ├── Pain relieved by leaning forward (pathognomonic)
  │   ├── Risk factors: gallstones, alcohol, hypertriglyceridaemia, ERCP, drugs, trauma
  │   └── Atypical: elderly may present with confusion, tachypnoea, or hypotension without classic pain
  ├── Laboratory (DIAGNOSTIC: lipase ≥3× ULN)
  │   ├── Lipase/amylase (lipase more specific and lasts longer)
  │   ├── LFTs (ALT >150 U/L strongly suggests gallstone aetiology — specificity 95%)
  │   ├── CRP (prognostic: >150 at 48h predicts severe disease)
  │   ├── Triglycerides (severe hypertriglyceridaemia >11 mmol/L can cause pancreatitis)
  │   ├── Calcium, LDH, glucose, urea, creatinine, ABG (Glasgow score components)
  │   └── Pregnancy test (in women of childbearing age)
  ├── Severity Assessment (within 48h of admission)
  │   ├── Glasgow (Imrie) Score (0-8): age>55, WBC>15, glucose>10, urea>16, PaO2<60, Ca<2, albumin<32, LDH>600
  │   │   └── ≥3 = predicted severe pancreatitis
  │   ├── CRP: >150 mg/L at 48h = predicted severe
  │   ├── Organ Failure Assessment: modified Marshall score (respiratory, renal, cardiovascular)
  │   │   └── Persistent organ failure >48h = severe pancreatitis (Revised Atlanta)
  │   └── SIRS criteria: ≥2 of HR>90, RR>20, temp <36 or >38, WBC <4 or >12
  ├── Imaging
  │   ├── Abdominal Ultrasound (within 24-48h): gallstones, CBD dilatation, peripancreatic fluid
  │   ├── CT Abdomen with IV contrast (≥72h after admission for prognostication)
  │   │   ├── Interstitial oedematous pancreatitis: diffuse/focal enlargement, homogeneous enhancement
  │   │   └── Necrotizing pancreatitis: non-enhancing areas of pancreas (lack of perfusion)
  │   └── MRCP/EUS: if suspected CBD stone not seen on US and cholangitis is absent
  └── Management by Severity
      ├── Mild (Revised Atlanta: no organ failure)
      │   ├── IV fluids (moderate: 1.5 mL/kg/h Ringer's Lactate)
      │   ├── Early oral feeding (low-fat soft diet as tolerated — NOT NBM)
      │   ├── Analgesia (Paracetamol + NSAIDs + opioids PRN)
      │   ├── No prophylactic antibiotics, no routine ERCP
      │   └── Same-admission cholecystectomy for gallstone pancreatitis (after biochemical resolution)
      ├── Moderate (transient organ failure <48h)
      │   ├── Ward admission with high-dependency monitoring
      │   ├── IV Ringer's Lactate (goal-directed: HR <120, MAP >65, UO >0.5 mL/kg/h, HCT 35-44%)
      │   ├── Nasoenteric feeding (start within 72h)
      │   ├── Monitor for organ failure progression
      │   └── CT at ≥72h to assess for necrosis
      └── Severe (persistent organ failure >48h / necrotizing)
          ├── ICU admission, organ support (ventilation, dialysis, vasopressors)
          ├── Goal-directed fluid resuscitation (monitor for fluid overload)
          ├── Nasoenteric feeding (start within 48-72h)
          ├── CT at ≥72h to assess necrosis
          ├── No prophylactic antibiotics — treat only confirmed infections
          ├── For infected necrosis: step-up approach (drainage → endoscopic/surgical necrosectomy)
          └── Gallstone aetiology: ERCP only if cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction

  ── East African Contextual Modifications ──
  • Gallstone pancreatitis is the most common cause — perform USS early
  • Ascaris infestation should be considered in young patients with no other risk factors
  • HIV-associated pancreatitis (drug-induced or infective) should be considered
  • Hypertriglyceridaemia is increasingly common — check fasting lipids
  • CT accessibility may limit objective assessment of necrosis — rely on clinical and laboratory markers
  • Step-up approach for infected necrosis may be limited by lack of endoscopic expertise — open necrosectomy is still performed
  • Same-admission cholecystectomy may be delayed if patient presents late (already developed organized necrosis)
  • Intensive care capacity limited — patients with predicted severe disease should be transferred early
  • Nutritional support is critical — enteral feeds via NG tube are feasible even in basic settings

managementPearls.context: Fluid resuscitation in pancreatitis — less is now more
managementPearls.pearl: The WATERFALL trial has changed how we resuscitate pancreatitis patients. The old dogma of 'aggressive fluid resuscitation' (5-10 L in the first 24h) is dead. Aggressive fluids increase mortality, ARDS, and abdominal compartment syndrome. My protocol now: (1) Use Ringer's Lactate (not Normal Saline — RL reduces hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis and SIRS). (2) Start with a 10 mL/kg bolus (about 700 mL in a 70 kg patient), then 1.5 mL/kg/h (about 100 mL/h). (3) Target: HR <120, MAP >65, urine output >0.5 mL/kg/h, haematocrit 35-44% (haemoconcentration above 44% suggests inadequate resuscitation; haemodilution below 35% suggests over-resuscitation). (4) Reassess every 4-6h and adjust. (5) In patients with renal failure or heart failure, be even more cautious. The most important lesson: if the patient looks fluid overloaded (rising CVP, peripheral oedema, increasing oxygen requirement), stop fluids and consider diuretics.
managementPearls.evidence: de-Madaria E, Buxbaum JL, Maisonneuve P, et al. Aggressive or moderate fluid resuscitation in acute pancreatitis. N Engl J Med. 2022;387(11):989-1000.

managementPearls.context: Timing of cholecystectomy in gallstone pancreatitis — the PONCHO trial
managementPearls.pearl: A patient with mild gallstone pancreatitis recovers in 3-5 days. The question is: when to remove the gallbladder? The PONCHO trial showed that same-admission cholecystectomy (after abdominal pain resolves and amylase/lipase normalizes) reduces recurrent gallstone events from 17% to 5% compared to interval surgery at 4-6 weeks. Waiting for interval surgery risks a second attack of pancreatitis (which may be severe) or cholecystitis in the interim. My practice: for mild pancreatitis (no necrosis, no organ failure), I perform laparoscopic cholecystectomy before discharge once the patient is eating and the abdominal pain has resolved — usually day 3-5. For severe pancreatitis with necrosis, I wait 6-8 weeks for the peripancreatic collections to resolve before cholecystectomy. For patients who cannot have surgery (too comorbid, too frail), ERCP + sphincterotomy alone reduces the risk of recurrent pancreatitis (but not cholecystitis).
managementPearls.evidence: da Costa DW, Bouwense SA, Schepers NJ, et al. Same-admission versus interval cholecystectomy for mild gallstone pancreatitis (PONCHO): a multicentre randomised controlled trial. Lancet. 2015;386(10000):1261-1268.

managementPearls.context: Infected pancreatic necrosis — when to intervene and when to wait
managementPearls.pearl: The most common mistake in managing necrotizing pancreatitis is intervening too early. Sterile necrosis does NOT need drainage or necrosectomy — it will absorb spontaneously in most patients over 6-12 weeks. Intervention is indicated ONLY for INFECTED necrosis. How to diagnose infected necrosis: (1) Clinical: persistent fever >38.5°C after 7-10 days from onset, rising WBC/CRP not explained by other sources. (2) CT: gas bubbles within the necrotic collection (pathognomonic for infection, but present in only 30%). (3) CT-guided fine-needle aspiration (FNA) for Gram stain and culture — the gold standard, but reserved for cases where the diagnosis is in doubt because of the risk of introducing infection into sterile necrosis. The PANTER trial taught us: when intervention is needed, start with percutaneous or endoscopic drainage (step 1). Only 50% of patients need progression to necrosectomy. Open necrosectomy should be the last resort, not the first.
managementPearls.evidence: van Santvoort HC, Besselink MG, Bakker OJ, et al. A step-up approach or open necrosectomy for necrotizing pancreatitis. N Engl J Med. 2010;362(16):1491-1502.

managementPearls.context: Nutrition in pancreatitis — feed the gut early
managementPearls.pearl: The era of 'pancreatic rest' (NBM + TPN) is over. Enteral nutrition, started within 48-72h of admission, reduces infections, organ failure, and mortality in predicted severe pancreatitis. How to feed: (1) In mild pancreatitis, start oral feeding immediately — low-fat soft diet, as tolerated. Do NOT keep the patient NBM. (2) In severe pancreatitis, place a nasogastric (NG) or nasojejunal (NJ) tube. The PYTHON trial showed that NG feeding is as safe and effective as NJ feeding — you do not need to place an NJ tube beyond the ligament of Treitz unless the patient has gastric outlet obstruction or persistent vomiting. (3) Start at 20-30 mL/h and advance by 10-20 mL/h every 4-6h as tolerated. (4) TPN is reserved for patients who cannot tolerate enteral feeds (<5% of cases). (5) If a patient has a symptomatic pseudocyst or duodenal compression from the inflamed pancreatic head, NG feeding may not be tolerated — in that case, consider NJ feeding or TPN. In our setting where TPN is difficult to source consistently, protecting the ability to feed enterally is critically important.
managementPearls.evidence: Bakker OJ, van Brunschot S, van Santvoort HC, et al. Early versus on-demand nasoenteric tube feeding in acute pancreatitis. N Engl J Med. 2014;371(21):1983-1993.

managementPearls.context: Ascaris-induced pancreatitis — a treatable cause in the tropics
managementPearls.pearl: In any young patient (<30) with acute pancreatitis and no gallstones or alcohol history, think of Ascaris lumbricoides. The worm migrates into the ampulla of Vater and causes pancreatic duct obstruction. Clues: (1) history of passage of worms per rectum, (2) eosinophilia, (3) ultrasound may show the worm (a tubular, echogenic structure with a central anechoic lumen — the 'inner tube sign') in the CBD or pancreatic duct. Diagnosis: stool microscopy for Ascaris ova, and ultrasound/ERCP visualization of the worm. Treatment: if the worm is in the duct at ERCP, extract it with a snare or basket. Then give albendazole 400 mg stat (or 400 mg daily for 3 days). In patients who do not need ERCP, treat with albendazole alone — the worm will die and pass. Prevention: deworming campaigns (annual albendazole/mebendazole) reduce the incidence significantly. Do not forget this diagnosis — it is eminently treatable and preventable.
managementPearls.evidence: Khuroo MS, Zargar SA, Mahajan R. Hepatobiliary and pancreatic ascariasis in India. Lancet. 1990;335(8704):1503-1506.

managementPearls.context: The 'forgotten' abdomen in the ICU — abdominal compartment syndrome in severe pancreatitis
managementPearls.pearl: In severe acute pancreatitis with massive fluid resuscitation, the intra-abdominal pressure (IAP) can rise to dangerous levels (abdominal compartment syndrome, ACS). An IAP >20 mmHg with new-onset organ failure defines ACS. How to monitor: measure intravesical pressure (bladder pressure) every 4-6h in any patient with predicted severe pancreatitis requiring >5 L of fluid in 24h. Signs of ACS: rising peak airway pressures, oliguria not responding to fluids, tense abdomen. Management: (1) Medical: evacuate intraluminal contents (NG tube + rectal tube), reduce fluid administration, consider neuromuscular blockade. (2) If IAP >25 mmHg with organ failure despite medical management: surgical decompression (midline laparostomy, leaving the abdomen open). (3) Do not close the abdomen under tension after laparotomy for pancreatitis complications — leave it open with a temporary closure. I have seen patients with pancreatitis and ACS who improved dramatically after decompression — it is a lifesaving manoeuvre that is underutilized.
managementPearls.evidence: Kirkpatrick AW, Roberts DJ, De Waele J, et al. Intra-abdominal hypertension and the abdominal compartment syndrome: updated consensus definitions and clinical practice guidelines from the WSACS. Intensive Care Med. 2013;39(7):1190-1206.

operativeNuances.procedure: Cholecystectomy in the Post-Pancreatitis Patient — Timing and Technical Considerations
operativeNuances.pitfall: Operating during the acute phase of severe pancreatitis (first 2 weeks) is dangerous — the tissues are oedematous, inflamed, and friable; dissection is hazardous and conversion rates are high
operativeNuances.expertTip: The timing of cholecystectomy after acute pancreatitis depends on severity: (1) Mild pancreatitis: operate during the SAME ADMISSION, after the abdominal pain resolves, the amylase/lipase normalizes, and the patient is tolerating diet. Usually day 3-7. Do not send the patient home to wait 4-6 weeks — they will have another attack. (2) Severe pancreatitis with necrosis: wait a minimum of 6-8 weeks. Perform a CECT before surgery to ensure peripancreatic collections have resolved. Operating with residual collections risks infecting them. (3) If the patient has a pseudocyst, wait until the cyst resolves or is drained endoscopically before cholecystectomy. (4) Technical tips: use a fundus-first (dome-down) approach if the Calot's triangle is inflamed; have a low threshold for IOC to define the anatomy; consider subtotal cholecystectomy if the anatomy is hostile. (5) In the patient with severe pancreatitis who has not had cholecystectomy, ERCP with sphincterotomy alone reduces but does not eliminate the risk of recurrent pancreatitis.

operativeNuances.procedure: Step-Up Approach — Percutaneous Drain Placement for Infected Necrosis
operativeNuances.pitfall: Placing a drain into a necrotic collection that is not yet liquefied — the drain will block and fail to drain
operativeNuances.expertTip: Timing is everything. Infected pancreatic necrosis becomes drainable only after 3-4 weeks when the necrotic tissue has undergone liquefaction and walled-off necrosis (WON) has formed. Before 3 weeks, the necrotic material is solid and will block any drain. After 4 weeks, a well-defined fibrous wall has formed ('walled-off pancreatic necrosis'). The optimal window is 4-6 weeks from onset. Technique for catheter drainage: (1) Use a posterior retroperitoneal approach (left flank) when possible — this avoids traversing the peritoneal cavity and the colon. (2) Use large-bore catheters (14-20 Fr) — small catheters will block. (3) Flush the drain with 20 mL saline twice daily to keep it patent. (4) If the drain blocks, advance a guidewire through the catheter, swap it for a larger drain, or consider adding a second drain. (5) If the patient does not improve within 48-72h of drainage (persistent fever, not clearing), proceed to necrosectomy. The step-up approach means: first step = drainage, but if that fails, go to the next step without delay.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Inadequate debridement leaving solid necrotic material behind, requiring reoperation
operativeNuances.expertTip: VARD is the 'step-up' surgical approach from the PANTER trial. Technique: (1) Position the patient in the right lateral decubitus position (right side down). (2) Make a 3-5 cm incision in the left flank at the site of the previously placed percutaneous drain. (3) Enter the retroperitoneal space through the drain tract — using the drain as a guide. (4) Insert a laparoscope or an operating mediastinoscope into the cavity. (5) Under direct vision, use long grasping forceps to remove pieces of necrotic tissue. Do not use suction alone — it will not remove solid necrosis. (6) Irrigate copiously with warm saline. (7) Leave two large-bore drains (24-28 Fr) in the cavity: one for irrigation (inflow) and one for drainage (outflow). (8) Plan repeat VARD procedures every 48-72h until the cavity is clear of necrosis and lined with healthy granulation tissue. Most patients need 2-4 procedures. (9) Do not try to remove all the necrosis at the first sitting — aggressive debridement risks bleeding from the peripancreatic vessels. Slow and steady wins the race.

operativeNuances.procedure: Open Necrosectomy — When the Step-Up Fails
operativeNuances.pitfall: Massive haemorrhage from the peripancreatic vessels during debridement — the splenic artery, SMA, SMV, and portal vein are all at risk
operativeNuances.expertTip: Open necrosectomy is the last resort, reserved for failure of the step-up approach. If you must do one: (1) Make a bilateral subcostal (chevron) incision or a midline laparotomy. (2) Enter the lesser sac through the gastrocolic ligament (between the stomach and the transverse colon). (3) Identify the necrotic pancreas — it will be a grey-black, liquefied mass with a 'cottage cheese' consistency. (4) Begin debridement with your fingers — blunt dissection is safer than sharp. The plane between viable and necrotic tissue is often easily separable by gentle finger dissection. (5) Bleeding during debridement: if you encounter bleeding, do not clamp blindly — compress with a pack for 5-10 minutes. Most bleeding is venous and will stop with pressure. If it does not stop, you have probably injured the splenic artery or a branch of the SMA — this requires direct suture repair under good visualization. (6) Do not attempt to remove all the necrosis — remove only the easily separable material. Overly aggressive debridement is the leading cause of bleeding and death. (7) Leave large drains and consider a feeding jejunostomy. (8) If there is extensive bleeding that cannot be controlled, pack the cavity and plan a second look in 48h. Open necrosectomy has a 30-50% mortality in most series — avoid it whenever possible.

operativeNuances.procedure: Pancreatic Pseudocyst — Endoscopic or Surgical Drainage
operativeNuances.pitfall: Attempting to drain a pseudocyst that is not mature (<6 weeks) or that does not have a well-formed wall — the drainage will leak and cause peritonitis
operativeNuances.expertTip: Pseudocysts should only be drained if they are: (1) symptomatic (pain, early satiety, obstructive jaundice), (2) infected, (3) enlarging, or (4) >6 cm and persistent beyond 6-8 weeks. The wall must be 'mature' — >5 mm thick on CT — to hold sutures or stents. Drainage options: (a) Endoscopic transmural drainage (cystogastrostomy or cystoduodenostomy) — the preferred approach when available. Use a linear echoendoscope to identify the cyst, puncture with a cautery-tipped catheter, dilate the tract, and place two double-pigtail stents and a nasocystic drain. (b) Laparoscopic cystogastrostomy — for cysts that bulge into the posterior gastric wall. Open the anterior stomach, identify the posterior wall bulge, open the cyst through the posterior gastric wall, and create a wide anastomosis with running 2-0 PDS suture. (c) Open cystojejunostomy — for cysts that do not reach the stomach or duodenum. A Roux-en-Y loop of jejunum is anastomosed to the cyst. In East Africa, open surgical drainage (cystogastrostomy or cystojejunostomy) is the most commonly available and effective approach.

prognosis.overallMortality: Overall mortality for acute pancreatitis: 2-5%. Interstitial oedematous pancreatitis: <1%. Necrotizing pancreatitis without infection: 5-10%. Infected pancreatic necrosis: 15-30%. Mortality is bimodal: early deaths (<2 weeks) from SIRS/MOF (40% of all deaths), late deaths (>2 weeks) from infected necrosis/sepsis (60% of all deaths). In East Africa, mortality is higher (5-10% overall, 20-40% for necrotizing) due to late presentation, limited ICU capacity, and lack of endoscopic step-up therapy. Mortality for severe pancreatitis requiring ICU: 25-35% in well-resourced centres, 40-60% in resource-limited settings.
prognosis.morbidity: Local complications: pancreatic necrosis 15-20%, infected necrosis 5-10%, pseudocyst 5-10%, pancreatic fistula 5%, splenic vein thrombosis 5-10%, bleeding pseudoaneurysm 2-5%. Systemic complications: ARDS 15-30%, AKI 20-30%, DIC 5-10%. Long-term sequelae: exocrine insufficiency (steatorrhoea requiring pancreatic enzyme replacement) 20-30% after necrotizing pancreatitis, endocrine insufficiency (diabetes) 15-30% after necrosis (new-onset diabetes correlates with extent of necrosis — >50% necrosis → 80% risk of diabetes). ICU stay: mean 2-4 weeks for severe disease. Hospital stay: mean 5-7 days for mild, 4-8 weeks for severe necrotizing.
prognosis.recurrence: After mild gallstone pancreatitis + same-admission cholecystectomy: <3% recurrence. After mild gallstone pancreatitis without cholecystectomy: 30-40% recurrence within 6 months. After alcohol-induced pancreatitis: 30-50% recurrence if drinking continues, <10% if abstinent. After hypertriglyceridaemia-induced pancreatitis: recurrence 20% if triglycerides are controlled with fibrates and dietary modification. After idiopathic pancreatitis: recurrence 15-20% (some may be due to microlithiasis not detected on initial imaging).
prognosis.survivalData: Five-year survival after mild acute pancreatitis: same as age-matched population. Five-year survival after severe necrotizing pancreatitis requiring ICU: 60-70% — survivors have reduced quality of life (physical domain scores, persistent pain, fatigue) and increased ongoing healthcare utilization. About 30% of survivors of severe pancreatitis develop chronic pancreatitis within 5 years. Ten-year risk of pancreatic cancer after acute pancreatitis: 2-3 fold increased risk compared to general population (most strongly associated with hereditary and tropical pancreatitis).
prognosis.landmarkReference: Banks PA, Bollen TL, Dervenis C, et al. Classification of acute pancreatitis — 2012: revision of the Atlanta classification and definitions by international consensus. Gut. 2013;62(1):102-111.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Mild/Interstitial (NON-SEVERE) vs Severe/Necrotizing (SEVERE) — persistent organ failure >48h defines severe pancreatitis
